segments.s050-g1: سپیده (dawn) is the subject placed before the clause marker چو, which here functions as the temporal conjunction 'when'. از تیره شب is a prepositional phrase 'from the dark night' indicating source. بردمید is an archaic past 3rd-person singular verb (poetic) meaning 'rose up/sprang up' and completes the temporal clause introduced by چو.
segments.s050-g2: میان شب تیره is a nominal phrase 'the dark midst of the night' (میان = middle/of the midst). اندر is a locative preposition 'in/within', and خمید is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'bent/stooped' used figuratively to say the darkness bowed or yielded as dawn appeared.
segments.s050-g3: منوچهر is the subject (proper name). برخاست is an archaic/plain past 3rd-person singular verb 'rose' or 'stood up'. از قلب‌گاه is a source/locative phrase 'from the heart-seat' (قلب‌گاه = seat of the heart; figurative centre or chamber), specifying where he rose from.
segments.s050-g4: This is an asyndetic list of accoutrements. ابا here functions as a prepositional/partitive element (in poetry often a variant of با or a cloak/robe), introducing the items that follow. جوشن (cuirass/armor), تیغ (sword) and رومی کلاه (literally 'Roman hat' = Roman helmet) are the objects; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. The adjective/ethnic marker رومی follows کلاه, forming 'a Roman helmet.'
segments.s050-g5: سپه is the subject 'army/host'. یکسره is an adverb 'all together/completely' modifying the action. نعره is the direct object 'a shout/cry' and برداشتند is the past 3rd-person plural verb 'they took up/raised'—here idiomatically 'they uttered/lifted (a shout)'.
segments.s050-g6: سنان‌ها (pl. 'spears') is topicalized at the start; به ابر اندر is a locative/goal phrase 'toward/into the clouds' (به ... اندر = into/against the cloud). افراشتند is a past 3rd-person plural verb 'they raised' (transitive), so the clause says the spears were raised up into the clouds. Note the poetic word order (object/topic before the verb).
segments.s050-g7: This nominal phrase is descriptive: پر از خشم = 'full of anger' (پر + از + noun). سر ابروان links 'head' and 'eyebrows' (a poetic enumeration), and پر ز چین means 'full of wrinkles/furrows' (پر = full, ز = poetic 'of', چین = wrinkle/fold). The whole phrase describes their angry, furrowed faces.
segments.s050-g8: همی is an older poetic particle indicating ongoing or emphatic action (here giving an immediacy to the verb). بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and نوشتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they wrote/inscribed' (used here either literally or figuratively). روی زمین 'upon the earth/ground' is the locative phrase receiving the action. The clause as a whole: 'they wrote/marked upon the ground.'
segments.s050-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شب روز شد is a simple past clause literally 'night became day' (i.e. dawn); شد is the past of شدن 'to become'. کس نیامد is a negative past-clause 'no one came' with کس 'no one' before the verb نیامد. به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the battle' marking the target or purpose of coming.
segments.s050-g34: دو here refers back to the two opponents ('the two of them'); جنگی is used substantively/adjectivally 'warlike' or 'a fight'. گرفتند is past simple 'they took' and forms a light-verb compound ('جنگی گرفتن' = 'to take up / begin a fight') meaning 'they engaged in fighting'. ساز درنگ is a terse poetic noun‑phrase (saz + darang) — literally 'the tune/strument of delay' — used here idiomatically to indicate manner ('without pause' or 'with no delay') so the whole segment conveys that the two immediately engaged in combat with no respite.
segments.s050-g25: همه چیرگی is a nominal phrase meaning 'complete dominion' (noun + modifier). با introduces the possessor/holder ('with Manuchehr') and بود is the past copula 'was', so the whole clause means 'all rule was with Manuchehr' (i.e. belonged to him).
segments.s050-g26: کزو is a relative/comparative particle 'from whom / from him' linking back to Manuchehr. مغز گیتی is a figurative construction 'the core/heart of the world' and پر از + noun means 'full of ...'. بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'from him the world's core was full of affection.'
segments.s050-g27: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. تا + temporal phrase gives 'until/when'; شب تیره 'dark night' is the noun + adjective. سر بر کشید is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to lift/raise the head' (سر 'head' + بر 'up/on' + کشید past of کشیدن), here used metaphorically of night 'rising'.
segments.s050-g28: درخشنده خورشید is 'the shining/brilliant sun' (adjective before noun). شد + predicate ناپدید means 'became unseen/disappeared' (شد as copular/becoming verb). The clause states the sun's disappearance as a resulting state.
segments.s050-g29: زمانه here means 'fortune / the course of events'; به یک سان is an adverbial phrase 'in one way / equally'. ندارد درنگ is 'does not have pause' — i.e. 'does not remain constant' or 'time does not stay the same.' Word order is Subject (زمانه) + adverbial (به یک سان) + verb (ندارد) + object (درنگ).
segments.s050-g30: گهی ... گاهی frames a contrast: 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. شهد و نوش است = 'there is honey and drink/delight' (است is the copula for the first part); the second part, گاهی شرنگ, gives the opposing possibility 'sometimes (there is) poison/poisonous draught.' نوش here functions as a noun 'drink'.
segments.s050-g31: دل تور و سلم is a possessive-like coordination 'the heart(s) of Tur and Salm'. اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came into' and به‌جوش is a predicate meaning 'into a boil/tumult' (figurative: became excited/angry). The construction thus says their hearts began to seethe.
segments.s050-g32: به راه 'on/along the road' (directional phrase), شبیخون 'night-raid', and نهادند (past of نهادن) 'they placed/set' take گوش as their object: نهادند گوش is a formulaic poetic expression 'they set their ears' meaning 'they listened intently' or 'kept watch/listened for' a night attack. Word order is verb-final (object before verb) as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s050-g17: This clause literal order is noun + copula + speech-verb: 'یکی بزمگاه‌ست گفتی' = 'you said, “one is a bāzmgāh (a hall of revelry).”' بزمگاه‌ست uses the poetic fused copula -ست (= is). گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' and here introduces reported speech; به‌جای is a preposition 'instead of / in place of' that links this clause to the following phrase.
segments.s050-g18: به‌جای ز ... is a two-part prepositional idea: 'in place of' followed by ز (poetic 'from/of'). شیپور is the trumpet/fanfare; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; نالیدن is the verbal-noun (gerund) 'wailing/laments'; کره نای is a genitive compound 'the young/offspring (کره) of the nāy (reed-flute)', a poetic image for the reed's plaintive cry. Together: 'in place of the trumpet and the reed's wailing.'
segments.s050-g19: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/they went away.' از جای 'from (their) place(s)' is the object phrase. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / at once' modifying the verb. چو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain' that follows the clause as a poetic comparison.
segments.s050-g20: دهاده is a quantifying word 'many / tenfold' modifying the verb برآمد 'rose up / emerged.' ز هر دو گروه is a prepositional phrase 'from each of the two groups' (ز = from, هر = each, دو = two, گروه = troop/band). The clause means that a multitude rose up from both groups.
segments.s050-g21: Subject + simile + verb + comment: بیابان is the subject 'the wilderness/desert.' چو دریای خون is a simile 'as / like a sea of blood' (دریای in construct with خون). شد is the past 'became.' درست is an adverb here meaning 'exactly / truly,' emphasizing that the change occurred as stated.
segments.s050-g22: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said.' که introduces the reported content 'that.' روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on the surface of the earth' (روی = surface / on). لاله رست is a simple nominal + verb phrase 'a tulip/tulips grew' (رست = sprouted/grew). Word order (subject before verb) is normal for classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s050-g23: پی here means 'after / following.' ژنده is an adjective 'tattered / ragged' modifying پیلان 'elephants.' به خون اندرون is a compound locative phrase 'into/with blood within' (به ... اندرون = into the interior in blood), and the clause is elliptical (no explicit finite verb here), a common poetic compression meaning essentially 'following the tattered elephants, (they) were drenched within with blood.'
segments.s050-g24: چنان چون is a double comparative 'so / thus as' introducing the likeness. ز بیجاده = 'from / out of a roadless place' (ز = from, بیجاده = roadless / off the beaten path). باشد is the (poetic) present/subjunctive of 'to be' used here to state an appearance or possibility ('might be / is'); ستون is the predicate noun 'pillar/column.' The line describes a pillar-like appearance, as if arising from a trackless place.
segments.s050-g9: A compact noun phrase listing the parts of the host. Persian allows a series of nouns joined by و (and) with the head noun سپاه at the end: “the left and right and core and wing of the army.” قلب is used here in the sense of 'core/center' rather than a literal heart.
segments.s050-g10: بیاراست is a past/participial form meaning 'was arrayed' or 'arrayed' and appears before لشکر in inverted poetic order; چو + finite verb (بایست) introduces a temporal clause 'when' (چو = when) and بایست here is an archaic/present-stem verb meaning 'stood' (i.e. 'when the king stood').
segments.s050-g11: Literal subject + copular verb: زمین شد = 'the earth became'. به کردار is a literary simile phrase 'in the manner/form of' (i.e. 'like'), followed by کشتی بر آب 'a ship on the water' (بر = on/upon). The whole clause means 'the earth took on the appearance/behaviour of a ship on the water.'
segments.s050-g12: تو گفتی is a simple reported-speech framing ('you said' / 'it was said by you'); گفتی here is the past speech verb (the provided noun senses do not match the verb sense). سوی غرق دارد شتاب is a verbless clause where دارد + noun ('has haste') functions like an intransitive predicate = 'hastens toward sinking' (سوی = toward; غرق = 'drowning/sinking'; شتاب = 'haste, speed').
segments.s050-g13: بزد is a past transitive verb 'he struck/smote'. مهره is the direct object (here a 'piece' / object struck — an exact reading is poetically vague). بر + کوهه marks location 'upon the mountain'. ژنده پیل is a noun phrase in apposition or adjectival phrase following the location: 'the ragged elephant' (ژنده used adjectivally).
segments.s050-g14: Compound predicate describing the earth's motion: زمین جنب‌جنبان uses the reduplicated adjective جنب‌جنبان 'trembling/to-and-fro' (continuous motion). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by دریای نیل 'the sea of the Nile' as the likeness. Word order places the simile after the adjectival description.
segments.s050-g15: همان پیش = 'right there/in front' or 'just before'. پیلان is 'elephants'. تبیره is an archaic/less-certain word in this position (not matched by the provided senses) and here functions as part of a verbal/participial phrase; زنان is the present-participle form 'striking/struck' used adjectivally ('—striking' or 'lashed'), modifying or describing the elephants. The phrase therefore presents the elephants performing a striking action immediately ahead.
segments.s050-g16: A coordinated string of adjectival/predicative words: خروشان and جوشان are participial/adjectival forms 'roaring' and 'boiling/seething' joined by و. پیلان دمان is a noun + genitival/relational word where دمان ('skirt, hem') here is used figuratively for the flank/rear; thus 'and the elephants of the skirts/skirts’ elephants (i.e. those at the rear).' The conjunctions simply link the descriptive images.

sentences.s050-s1: When dawn rose from the dark night, the dark midst of the night bent inward.
sentences.s050-s2: Manuchehr rose from the seat of the heart, clad in (or with) cuirass, sword, and a Roman helmet.
sentences.s050-s3: The entire host gave a single cry; their spears they raised into the clouds.
sentences.s050-s4: With heads full of anger and brows full of furrows, they inscribed (or marked) the face of the earth.
sentences.s050-s17: When night turned to day no one came to the battle; the two of them took up the fight without pause.
sentences.s050-s13: All dominion belonged to Manuchehr; from him the very core of the world was full of affection.
sentences.s050-s14: Thus, when the dark night lifted its head, the brilliant sun became unseen.
sentences.s050-s15: Fortune does not remain the same; sometimes there is honey and drink, and at other times (there is) poison.
sentences.s050-s16: The hearts of Tur and Salm boiled; they set their ears along the road for a night-raid.
sentences.s050-s9: You said one was a hall of revelry, in place of the trumpet and the plaintive wailing of the ney.
sentences.s050-s10: They all departed from their places at once; like a mountain, many arose from both bands.
sentences.s050-s11: The wilderness became like a sea of blood, exactly as you said; you said that tulips grew on the surface of the earth.
sentences.s050-s12: Following the tattered elephants, (they) were within blood; thus, as if from a roadless place, (they) stood like a pillar.
sentences.s050-s5: The army's left, right, center and flanks were arrayed when the king took his stand.
sentences.s050-s6: The earth became like a ship upon the water; you said it hastens toward drowning.
sentences.s050-s7: He struck a piece upon the mountain, (the) ragged elephant; the earth reeled to and fro like the Nile.
sentences.s050-s8: Right before (them) the elephants—striking/lashed—roaring and seething, and the elephants at the skirts (the rear) ...
